---
title: "Stochastic SIR dynamics"
subtitle: "Working through the math together"
author: "John Drake"
institute: "Odum School of Ecology"
event: "Population Biology Group Seminar"
location: "Ecology 403, University of Georgia"
date: today
format:
  drake-charcoal-revealjs:
    slide-number: c/t
    transition: none
    incremental: false
---

# Setup {.divider}

## What we want to understand

The deterministic SIR model is clean, but real outbreaks are noisy.
Today we will build up the stochastic version and see where the
deterministic picture breaks.

- When does stochasticity matter most?
- What does "extinction" mean in a stochastic model?
- How do we simulate this efficiently?

## The deterministic backbone

The classic SIR system:

$$
\frac{dS}{dt} = -\frac{\beta S I}{N}, \quad
\frac{dI}{dt} = \frac{\beta S I}{N} - \gamma I, \quad
\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma I
$$

This gives us the epidemic curve everyone knows. But it assumes
infinitely many individuals.

### What happens when $N$ is small?

## Transition rates

In the stochastic (continuous-time Markov chain) formulation,
we replace derivatives with event rates:

| Event       | Rate             | Change            |
|-------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Infection   | $\beta S I / N$  | $S-1$, $I+1$     |
| Recovery    | $\gamma I$       | $I-1$, $R+1$     |

Each event is a single individual changing compartment.

## Dreissenid mussel invasions {.figure}

![](figures/fig-invasion-curves-1.png){fig-align="center"}

<p class="caption">Newly invaded lakes (left) and cumulative invaded lakes (right) for *D. polymorpha* and *D. bugensis* in North America.</p>

# Simulation {.transition}

Building intuition through code

## Gillespie simulation

```r
gillespie_sir <- function(beta, gamma, S0, I0, tmax) {
  S <- S0; I <- I0; R <- 0; t <- 0
  out <- data.frame(t = t, S = S, I = I, R = R)

  while (t < tmax && I > 0) {
    rate_inf <- beta * S * I / (S + I + R)
    rate_rec <- gamma * I
    rate_tot <- rate_inf + rate_rec

    dt <- rexp(1, rate_tot)
    t  <- t + dt

    if (runif(1) < rate_inf / rate_tot) {
      S <- S - 1; I <- I + 1
    } else {
      I <- I - 1; R <- R + 1
    }
    out <- rbind(out, data.frame(t, S, I, R))
  }
  out
}
```

## Stochastic fadeout {.figure-slide}

![](figures/stochastic-fadeout.png){fig-align="center"}

<p class="caption">Twenty stochastic SIR realizations ($N = 100$, $R_0 = 2$). Several epidemics die out before reaching the deterministic peak.</p>

## Key insight

> In small populations the epidemic can go extinct
> by chance, even when $R_0 > 1$.

This is *stochastic fadeout* --- and it has no analogue in the
deterministic model.

## Where stochasticity matters

Three regimes to keep in mind:

- **Early invasion** ($I$ small) --- fate of the outbreak is a coin flip
- **Near the peak** ($I$ large) --- deterministic model is a good guide
- **Tail phase** ($I$ declining) --- last cases are again stochastic

This is why importation events are so consequential: each one
restarts the early-invasion lottery.

## A natural example {background-image="figures/field.jpg" background-size="cover"}

::: {.frosted}
Amphibian chytrid dynamics in small, isolated populations where
census sizes are in the tens --- exactly the regime where
stochasticity dominates.
:::

## Working group {.collaborators}

::: {.people}

::: {.person}
![](figures/person1.png)

Erica Laber
:::

::: {.person}
![](figures/person2.png)

Tobias Brett
:::

::: {.person}
![](figures/person3.png)

Paige Miller
:::

::: {.person}
![](figures/person4.png)

Éric Marty
:::

:::

## {.full-image background-image="figures/photo.png" background-size="cover" background-position="center"}

::: {.full-image-label}
Full image slide
:::

## Reading {.watermark background-image="figures/watermark.png" background-size="contain" background-position="center" background-opacity="0.10"}

For next time, review Chapter 6 of Keeling & Rohani on
stochastic models, paying attention to the relationship between
population size and fadeout probability.

# Questions? {.divider}

*Let's discuss.*
